title: Thinking Biblically About Life: Technology
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 1-5-25 - Thinking Biblically About Life 7 - Technology - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Technology Is Not Just Smartphones
subpoints: Technology is everywhere and is the result of being created in God's image — we create because God creates
Technology is not always appropriately used

Three recommended resources: "Truth in Love" podcast ep. 496 (Spencer Harmon); "Digital Liturgies" by Samuel D. James (cautious/negative perspective); "12 Ways Your Phone Is Changing You" by Tony Reinke (more positive)
The iPhone launched in 2007; the next generation has never known life without it

outline.title: How Does Technology Affect Our Spiritual Lives?
subpoints: Can waste time; can help use time more effectively (e.g., listening while driving)
Can become a crutch that replaces thinking and meditating on God
Provides easy access to Scripture and resources; also distracts from deep reflection
Can crowd our thoughts and enslave us (like an addiction)
Can simulate relationships and give a false sense of connection
Makes knowledge broad and shallow rather than deep and specific
Is changing us — the key question is how

outline.title: Nine Theological Statements About Technology
subpoints: 1. Technology modifies creation — subdues it for human good and efficiency; evidence of the image of God
2. Technology pushes back the results of the Fall (antibiotics, pain relief, clothing, crop improvement)
3. Technology establishes human power — unmoored from God, it quickly becomes a weapon for domination
4. Technology helps to identify and include souls — from stone tablets to paper to digital Bible distribution
5. Technology upholds and empowers our bodies — but we can trust it instead of God who gave it
6. Technology gives voice to human autonomy — the Tower of Babel is the paradigm case (Gen. 11)
7. God governs every human technology — the cross is the supreme example: man's instrument of death became the center of redemption
8. Technology shapes every relationship — communication technology has accelerated from the Pony Express to instant text messages
9. Technology shapes our theology — our view of God is affected by our technological context

outline.title: How to Discern the Use of Technology: The Decision-Making Triangle
subpoints: Central question: How should I use technology?
Corner 1 — Glorify God: "Whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God" (1 Cor. 10:31)
Corner 2 — Receive it as a gift: "Every creature of God is good and nothing is to be refused if it is received with thanksgiving" (1 Tim. 4:4-5)
Corner 3 — Do good with it: "Love your neighbor as yourself" (Luke 10:27); e.g., using technology to coordinate meals for those in need

outline.title: Five Quick Applications
subpoints: 1. Thoughtlessly adopting new technology is worldliness — not thinking = being conformed to the world
2. Technology is technology — avoid the error of thinking today's tech is bad but yesterday's was fine
3. Whatever your smartphone is doing to you, it is also pointing you toward a glorious city to come — nothing satisfies completely, pointing us to eternity
4. All these realities are changing us — the bigger question is how
5. We cannot idolize mobile technology — Psalm 115:8: those who worship idols become like them; take regular waking-hour breaks from digital technology

summary: This final lesson in the "Thinking Biblically About Life" series addresses technology. Pastor Tito Lyro begins by establishing that technology is not a new or narrowly digital phenomenon — it encompasses any use of creation to serve human purposes, and it is evidence that we bear the image of the Creator God. He recommends three resources representing a spectrum of perspectives: the Truth in Love podcast (ep. 496), Samuel D. James's "Digital Liturgies" (cautious), and Tony Reinke's "12 Ways Your Phone Is Changing You" (balanced/positive). The core of the lesson is nine theological statements about technology: it modifies creation, pushes back the Fall's curse, can entrench human power, aids the spread of souls, empowers bodies but risks idolatry, amplifies human autonomy (Tower of Babel), is governed by God, shapes every relationship, and shapes theology. He then offers Spencer Harmon's decision-making triangle — glorify God, receive it as a gift, use it to do good — as a practical discernment tool. The lesson concludes with five quick applications, including the warning that thoughtlessly adopting new technology is worldliness and that idolizing our devices will make us as mute as the idols of Psalm 115.
transcript:
## Introduction: Technology Is Not Just Smartphones

This is our seventh and final lesson in the "Thinking Biblically About Life" series. We have considered church membership, trauma and suffering, gentle parenting, adoption (two sessions), morbid introspection, and helping post-abortive people. And without planning it, a book our congregation recently read on humility — which included an epilogue on humility in social media — provided a perfect segue into today's topic: thinking biblically about technology.

Technology is all around us. We tend to think of technology as just our smartphones or computers or some advanced gadget, but technology is everywhere. It is not just the newest thing. I love the fact that we have antibiotics so that we are not generally dying of infections anymore. There is hope if you get a cut. It is important for us to realize that technology is the result of being human. We are created in God's image, and God is a creative being — He is the Creator. Because of that, we also create.

Yet I think we all agree that technology is not always appropriately used. And so it is important to think biblically about technology.

Three resources to help you think more about this area:

1. The **Truth in Love podcast**, Episode 496, "Shepherding Families in Technology," with Spencer Harmon. It dropped December 16, 2024. The Truth in Love podcast is generally excellent — usually 15-20 minutes. Dale Johnson, the executive director of the Association of Certified Biblical Counselors, interviews guests on issues of life and counseling.

2. **Digital Liturgies** by Samuel D. James (Crossway, 2023). This author is very cautious about digital technology. The first chapter takes work, but then it becomes an easy read. Very good.

3. **12 Ways Your Phone Is Changing You** by Tony Reinke (2017). He tends to see the glass as more half-full. The principles are still very helpful despite the book's age.

## How Does Technology Affect Our Spiritual Lives?

Let us think about this question together. Technology can waste time instead of helping us be productive. It can also help us use time better — listening to edifying material while driving or working. It can become a crutch that answers our questions instead of meditating on God. It makes Bible search much easier. It can crowd our minds and prevent deep thought. It can simulate relationships and give a false sense of connection. It can enslave us — like returning again and again to something destructive. It can help us see our own hearts by showing us what we gravitate toward. It makes knowledge broad and shallow rather than deep and specific.

All of these answers suggest one thing: technology is changing us. But also notice that we immediately jump to mobile technology instead of thinking of technology as a whole. Who drove a horse and buggy here today? We benefit from many older technologies too, right?

The iPhone launched in August 2007. Those born after 2007 have lived their entire lives with smartphones present. My generation is the last to have grown up without the internet. The next generation has been raised with it always around. So it is good to have a cross-sectional understanding, to avoid both the utopian optimism of the technophile and the dystopian pessimism of the technophobe. We do not want to be guilty of what C.S. Lewis called chronological snobbery — the assumption that what is new is always good and what is old is always bad.

## A Theology of Technology: Nine Statements

**First: Technology modifies creation.** Technology is used to subdue creation for human good and to increase efficiency. Technology is the reordering of raw materials for human purposes — evidence that we bear the image of the Creator God.

**Second: Technology pushes back the results of the Fall.** What were the results of the Fall? Hard labor, pain in childbearing, thorns and thistles, death. Consider: farmers use technology to minimize weeds. Women use pain-suppressing technology in childbirth. Fashion designers use fabric to cover our bodies. Technology is a gracious gift from God helping us live in this fallen creation. But all of this technology also reminds us of our fundamental problem — we are alienated from God — and we try to solve that through technology, through substances, through every means except turning to God.

**Third: Technology establishes human power.** Unmoored from the fear and obedience of God, technology quickly becomes a pawn in human power plays. Almost immediately after German scientists discovered how to split the atom, the technology was weaponized. One nation could subjugate another.

**Fourth: Technology helps to identify souls.** As God planned to move from a land-based religion (Old Testament) to a going-into-all-the-world religion (New Testament), chisel and stone gave way to primitive advances in paper and ink, making written communication possible. Technology has always been a driver of gospel transmission — from stone to treated leather to wood products to paper to digital screens.

**Fifth: Technology upholds and empowers our bodies.** It enhances our bodies, refines our movements, shapes how we present ourselves to the world. But we can easily trust the technology rather than the Lord who gave it. That is why we read Psalm 115 today — the most current technology can become that god after which we are shaped. Psalm 115:8 warns: those who worship false idols will become like them.

**Sixth: Technology gives voice to human autonomy.** The key biblical case study is the Tower of Babel (Genesis 11) — an attempt to consolidate all known building innovations to build a rebel city, consolidating power and saying, "We will reach up to God." And we see God stooping down just to see this greatest of human inventions, almost mockingly. Technology is a platform for the fantasy of human autonomy — the original temptation of Genesis 3: "You shall be like gods."

**Seventh: God governs every human technology.** The best example is the cross. God created trees to serve humanity. Humans turned trees into an instrument of death and pain. And then God used that technology as the crux — the very center of human redemption — overriding the purpose for which men had created it.

**Eighth: Technology shapes every relationship.** Every technology changes the fundamental social dynamics of how we relate to the world, to one another, and to God. Communication technology has accelerated from the Pony Express (news in two weeks from a thousand miles away) to the instant text message. Now we cannot wait two minutes for a response — especially if the message is marked as read.

**Ninth: Technology shapes our theology.** Our view of God is affected by the technological context in which we live. We need to be aware of this.

## How to Discern the Use of Technology: The Decision-Making Triangle

I am drawing here on Spencer Harmon's suggestion of a decision-making triangle — though it can serve as a discernment tool for anything in life.

At the center: **How should I use technology?**

At the top corner: **Glorify God.** Write down 1 Corinthians 10:31: "Therefore, whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God."

At the second corner: **Receive it as a gift.** Write down 1 Timothy 4:4-5: "Every creature of God is good, and nothing is to be refused if it is received with thanksgiving, for it is sanctified by the word of God and prayer." We cannot be reactionary — even the car we drove here was a product of technology. GPS means we rarely get lost anymore. These are enormous blessings received as gifts.

At the third corner: **Do good with it.** Luke 10:27: "Love your neighbor as yourself." How can I use this technology to bless other people? Phone calls, resources to share, tools to help with projects, enjoying God's creation... even using a sign-up website to coordinate meals for people who have babies or are sick — that is using technology to love your neighbor.

All of this is under the umbrella of the glory of God. It is not a complex issue at its root — it simplifies to: glorify God, receive the gifts He gives with thanksgiving, and use them to do good.

## Five Quick Applications

**First: Thoughtlessly adopting new technology is worldliness.** If you are not thinking about what you are doing, you are being conformed to the world. That is the definition of worldliness.

**Second: Technology is technology.** We should not think that today's tech is inherently bad while yesterday's was fine. That is just adopting what the world does without thinking.

**Third: Whatever your smartphone is doing to you, it is also pointing you toward a glorious city to come.** Whatever fulfillment — or lack thereof — it provides, technology will always leave you wanting something more. Your heart is made for something else: for God, and for the life to come.

**Fourth: All these realities are changing us.** There is no debate about that. The bigger question is how, and we should be concerned about that.

**Fifth: We cannot idolize mobile technology.** Psalm 115:8: those who worship idols become like them — dumb, deaf, blind. One way to check our relationship with technology is to take regular waking-hour breaks from digital devices. Sleeping does not count.
